exploration_log.aha_moment: The cleanup (`registration.Dispose()` then `_cancellationTokenSourcePool.Return(...)`) runs sequentially with no `finally`. If `Dispose()` throws, `Return` is skipped and the token source leaks. Wrapping in try/finally guarantees Return runs regardless.
exploration_log.hindsight: No dead ends; the fix location was exactly as described in the task.

alternatives: Wrap both in a single finally by using a dummy try{} finally{Dispose; Return}
Use try{Dispose} finally{Return} so Return is guaranteed
key_decisions.reasoning: This is the minimal structural change that guarantees Return is always called, even if Dispose throws. Both cleanup steps are now part of the try/finally construct.

solve_notes: Read `TimeoutResilienceStrategy.ExecuteCore` and found that `registration.Dispose()` and `_cancellationTokenSourcePool.Return(cancellationSource)` execute sequentially outside any `finally`. Wrapped them in `try { registration.Dispose(); } finally { _cancellationTokenSourcePool.Return(cancellationSource); }` to guarantee the pool return even if Dispose throws.
task_text:
Fix TimeoutResilienceStrategy cleanup not protected by finally block

`TimeoutResilienceStrategy.ExecuteCore` acquires a
`CancellationTokenSource` from `_cancellationTokenSourcePool` and
registers a `CancellationTokenRegistration` for cleanup. However,
the cleanup sequence — `registration.Dispose()` followed by
`_cancellationTokenSourcePool.Return(cancellationSource)` — executes
outside any `finally` block. If an unexpected exception escapes the
cleanup sequence, the token source is leaked from the pool. Fix the
strategy to wrap both `registration.Dispose()` and
`_cancellationTokenSourcePool.Return(cancellationSource)` in a
`finally` block in `TimeoutResilienceStrategy.cs`, guaranteeing
cleanup under all execution paths.
